item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never thought I'd see two Grim Reapers and a Foundation agent engage in some wholesome WLW-MLM solidarity
Also god dammit, I only just now got that pun at the end
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well they are a romantic couple on vacation, also because they are Grim Reapers I'd bet they do some kinky stuff that doesn't need to be mentioned in an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The SCP universe is full of horrors. Machine gods, torture telephones, omnicidal lizards, surgery devices that operate on souls, titanic snakes that eat memories and pee forget-me-now juice.

But sometimes, the weird is just…. weird.

There is no gimmick, no horror, no catch. This SCP is just a lame couple on a lame vacation enjoying a cheesy holiday.

I love this kind of thing. Chances are I will have forgotten it by tomorrow but isn't that life? Is the road of life not 80% boring, forgettable stuff?

Grats on the +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, they'd probably make a living by doing restaurant reviews or travel guides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find myself wondering what SCP-3540-1 and SCP-3540-2 would do in the End of Death canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple of very minor grammatical errors in the log I noticed:

typically from cities in Southern US states.

Sounds redundant, I think "cities in the Southern United States" works better here

there does not

do not*

Sorry if that sounds nitpicky, this was a nice article about a vacation for the Spectres of Death, +1 :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was overly corny, and the concept is pretty stupid.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is making me think about the idea of Level II Psychopomp Entities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ultimately didn't classify this as Keter, although your arguments are correct, as I felt that since they communicated with and were generally amiable with the Foundation, and since the criteria is so specific, that it wasn't super unpredictable. It was kinda on the edge between Euclid and Keter, and I wanted to err on the side of the less extreme class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really nice lighthearted skip that doesn't get too silly and still feels like a skip, while making me smile. One side note: honestly, this doesn't often get said, but this should be Keter. It's not actively dangerous, sure, but it's absurdly difficult (and expensive) to contain due to unpredictability and untraceability, and that's what defines classification. I understand if you absolutely feel that Keter classification undermines the tone of the article, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a while to get the expunged part, but then I remembered that these two are a couple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never thought two grim reapers arguing with each other over Halloween decorations could work, but you made it happen. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely charming, +1.

The idea is strangely comforting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to know what that expunged is.
Honestly, I was one edge for the entire addendum because I thought that at one point, either when Kay told them about their decorations violating property or some other time, I was sure as Hell that something bad was gonna happen. Like, this unknown anomalous property was going to result in them going berserk and cooking the agent for dinner or something. But they didn’t, which is a relief. Nice job author keeping the article wholesome and good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was, not hyperbole, baffling the first time I read through it. Not because I didn't understand. Just the image of a pair of psychopomps living in the suburbs like there's nothing unusual going on, carrying on a conversation about Halloween decorations and chores, made my brain do a backflip.

The only gripe I have is the [DATA EXPUNGED] at the end of the list of mundane household chores. That instantly sent my mind to the gutter, unless the Foundation is just so jaded on what they consider are "common activities" really aren't, and it was only got caught by a peer reviewer after the draft was submitted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think they have some really freaky fetish in that expunged segment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, if a particular narrative element breaks suspension of disbeleif, then I don't see why that's a problem. If one is enjoying a work and is taken out of the story due to something like that, to the point where they no longer enjoy it, then yea, a downvote could be the result of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have a point about expungement vs. redaction, but you downvoted just because of that one thing? Really?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'Death as a cute gay couple who love Halloween' sounds like such a stupid idea, but you pulled it off.

The bickering about who told who to decorate where cracked me up. They sound like my parents.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Precisely. As I noted above, it was a cheeky redaction, but I felt it worked well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
after sunset, they will manifest in various parts of the house and will engage in typical household chores and common activities, including cleaning, preparing and consuming meals, folding laundry, and [DATA EXPUNGED].

So I get from this list, and the later details, that, as a married romantic couple on vacation, they are F—-ing. I'd actually consider this a perfect use of [DATA EXPUNGED]. At first, it seems sinister, but the latter details leave the reader to fill in an action.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always gathered that redaction is removing what isn't need to know for general public , whereas expungements are made for removing info that would normally be visible to personnel at that Clearance Level.

I also don't get the need for the expungement. If it is a common household activity it wouldn't be expunged, it would be redacted (imo) or not removed at all.Could just be me andmy headcanon tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Expunging data is a terrible habit. It's not just a synonym for redaction. What happens when it becomes vitally important to know what the entities do, and now nobody knows because it was expunged instead of redacted.

By and large redaction and expunging data serve the same purpose out of universe. In universe, there is a difference, but they both do the same thing. They beg the question of what was behind the redaction. I believe your perspective here is artificially limiting. The difference between the two types of redcation are negligible except for stories where not having that information is key. For a simple, light-hearted SCP, it doesn't matter all that much.

2. Censoring the last item in a list is a terrible trope. It has its place, like listing the negative effects in order of severity, but is the last item in this list severely ordinary?

It's really not a terrible trope. Censoring the last item on a list helps with flow of reading, which is fairly important to me as a writer. You also clearly didn't pay very much attention to how I ordered the items within my list. I go from the most broad activity and narrow the scope and focus of each activity after that, until the final item on the list. And again, your belief that the only applicable time that the last item on a list should be censored is when the list is in order of severity is artificially limiting.

I will fully admit it is a cheeky redaction. But this article is not a self-serious, grimdark article from Series 2. It's a punchline to a joke and for that it's perfectly fine. It's the writing equivalent of a beat between lines.

3. Har har, it's a euphemism for ███████ ████ █████ ██ ███ ███. So just say that they're ███████ ████ █████ ██ ███ ███. At least then there's presumably a copy of the document available at the appropriate security level, in case some O5 really really needs to know that Maurice and Lance like to ████ ████ █████ ██ ███ ███.

Again, this falls back into readability. Blackboxes are fundamentally an ugly character. And filling an entire line with black boxes just creates a giant dead space within the page that distracts the reader and harms the readability of the rest of the article because of it.

There is a time and place for the use of redactions, and where I used a redaction in my article was an appropriate time and place. I'm honestly kind of insulted that you believe I did not put thought into the words I chose and the placement of my redactions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The entities cannot be physically seen during the day, but after sunset, they will manifest in various parts of the house and will engage in typical household chores and common activities, including cleaning, preparing and consuming meals, folding laundry, and [DATA EXPUNGED]."

1. Expunging data is a terrible habit. It's not just a synonym for redaction. What happens when it becomes vitally important to know what the entities do, and now nobody knows because it was expunged instead of redacted.

2. Censoring the last item in a list is a terrible trope. It has its place, like listing the negative effects in order of severity, but is the last item in this list severely ordinary?

3. Har har, it's a euphemism for ███████ ████ █████ ██ ███ ███. So just say that they're ███████ ████ █████ ██ ███ ███. At least then there's presumably a copy of the document available at the appropriate security level, in case some O5 really really needs to know that Maurice and Lance like to ████ ████ █████ ██ ███ ███.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not even gonna lie. That pun was completely unintended when I was writing it. It’s just such a suburban thing to say. But it fit waaaaaaay to well with the draft that I just had to leave it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She stumbles over her words a lot. That was an intentional choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This a great tale in the making, I really, really love the charm in this.

Small thing though Gittings says I need to head out twice in his last line.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All this silliness, but then Lance and Maurice want to adopt but can't. And then the pun. Whiplash.

÷1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Lance can make chicken parmesan that's to die for."

*GROAN*

+1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
"Cover that shit in web".

Near instant +1. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite enjoyed this. The interplay between the entities and the Foundation personnel was very well handled; I like it when exposition is Incorporated into dialogue and narrative.

Also, having a Halloween themed SCP in April is positively refreshing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dawwwwwww

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this resonates with me, especially since Halloween is the only holiday I have mainly fond memories of, since I spend that time with my friends usually. The other holidays are mostly awkward forced family-time involving too much food because I eat to avoid having to talk to people asking me too-personal questions. I like the vibe this articles gives off.

Also, I thought this part:

Gittings: Actually, uh, speaking of the decorations. I actually came over here to tell you that you need to remove some of your decorations. One of your inflatables and a bunch of your cobwebs are in your neighbors' properties.

Both entities appear to look at each other for several seconds.

was absolutely hilarious.

Wholehearted +1 from me for this wholeheartedly wholesome article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a huge sucker for ghosts/supernatural beings of any kind, but this is especially endearing and I like that. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can understand your opinion that the idea was rather bland, but I hope my author post clears up some of the blandness at all.

I would also appreciate if you could point out my spelling mistakes. I was pretty sure I'd gotten all of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like each individual component (authentic Halloween house and the couple) but I'm not keen on the two together. I feel like the connection is hardly there. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite a couple of spelling mistakes, plus, the scp idea is really bland.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is a story about the dead. Or more specifically, the people who lead the dead to whatever lies beyond. This was inspired by a coldpost I saw several years ago about a person who appeared in abandoned houses during Halloween and killed the children who came to the house. I wanted to invert that idea, so I wrote a pair of grim reapers who take the month of October off to celebrate the season that is the most relevant to them.

I was actully kinda inspired by Ingmar Bergman's film Seventh Seal and it's rather casual portrayal of the death figure. I wanted to take my own crack at it, but with a decidedly more lighthearted take on it. Fear of death is intrinsic to being a human, so I wanted to show how human Death truly is.

Thank you to Shaggydredlocks, Fieldstone, Captain Kirby, and Ratsy for feedback.

+ show block
– hide block

Image Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Weatherly_PA_Halloween_house.jpg
